transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Morgan, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I’m not really sure. I mean, there’s always a lot going on at work, but I don’t know if it’s worth talking about. Sometimes I just feel like people are... well, they’re talking about me behind my back. It’s probably nothing, but it gets to me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like work has been a bit overwhelming for you, and you're feeling some uncertainty about how others perceive you. It’s understandable to feel that way, especially if you sense that people might be talking about you. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you feel that way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know, it’s just... sometimes I catch people whispering or laughing when I walk by. It feels like they’re saying something about me. I try not to think about it too much, but it just sticks in my mind. I guess I worry that they think I’m not good enough or something. But I mean, it’s probably just my imagination, right?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to feel that way, especially when you notice people whispering or laughing. It’s natural to worry about how others perceive us, and it seems like this has been weighing on your mind. You mentioned feeling like they might think you’re not good enough. Can you share more about where that feeling comes from for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess it’s just... I’ve always felt a bit out of place. Like, I don’t really fit in with my coworkers. They seem to have this easy rapport, and I struggle to connect with them. So, when I see them together, it makes me think they must be saying something negative about me. I don’t know if that’s true, but it’s hard not to feel that way sometimes. It just makes me anxious, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like feeling out of place and struggling to connect with your coworkers is really contributing to your anxiety. That sense of not fitting in can be really isolating, especially when you see others having an easy rapport. It’s understandable that this would lead you to worry about what they might be saying. How do you usually cope with those feelings of anxiety when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I tend to just... think about it a lot. I replay conversations in my head, trying to figure out what I might have done wrong or if I said something that upset them. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop, and it doesn’t really help. Sometimes I just try to distract myself with work or something else, but it’s hard to shake off those thoughts. I guess I don’t really have a good way to cope with it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re caught in a cycle of overthinking and replaying interactions, which can be really exhausting. It makes sense that you’d want to distract yourself from those thoughts, but it seems like they still linger. It’s tough when you feel like you don’t have effective ways to cope with that anxiety. What do you think would help you break that loop, even just a little?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m not sure, honestly. I guess if I could just... feel more confident about myself or something, maybe it wouldn’t bother me as much. But it’s hard to imagine that. Sometimes I think if I could just talk to someone about it, maybe it would help, but I’m not really good at opening up. I just don’t want to burden anyone with my worries.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re really yearning for that confidence and connection, but it feels challenging to open up about your worries. It’s understandable to worry about burdening others, but sharing what you’re going through can sometimes lighten that load. You’re not alone in feeling this way. What do you think might help you feel more comfortable opening up, even just a little?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess if I felt like the person I was talking to really understood or wouldn’t judge me, maybe I could share a bit more. But it’s hard to trust that. I mean, I don’t really know you that well yet. I just... I don’t want to come across as weak or anything. It feels safer to keep things to myself. But I also know that holding it all in isn’t helping me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes a lot of sense that you’d want to feel understood and free from judgment before sharing more. Trust can take time to build, and it’s okay to take things at your own pace. It sounds like you’re aware of the struggle between wanting to keep things in and recognizing that it’s not helping you. What do you think might help you feel a little safer in sharing, even if it’s just a small piece of what you’re experiencing?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe if I could just start with something small, like a specific moment or feeling, it wouldn’t feel so overwhelming. I guess I could try to talk about what happened at work last week when I felt really anxious. It’s just... I don’t want to dive too deep too fast, you know? But I think starting small might help.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting small sounds like a great approach, and it’s perfectly okay to take your time. I appreciate your willingness to share a specific moment. What happened at work last week that made you feel anxious?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, there was this meeting, and I had to present my project. I thought I was prepared, but when I started talking, I noticed a few people whispering to each other. I couldn’t help but feel like they were judging me or thinking I was messing up. I got really nervous and stumbled over my words. Afterward, I just kept replaying it in my head, thinking about how I must have looked or what they might have said. It made me feel really anxious for days.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a really challenging experience, especially being in a situation where you felt unprepared and noticed others whispering. It’s understandable that it would stick with you and lead to those feelings of anxiety. It seems like that moment had a significant impact on how you viewed yourself and your work. How did you feel about your presentation afterward, aside from the anxiety? Did you have any thoughts about your performance?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Honestly, I felt pretty embarrassed. I kept thinking about how I could have done better and how I should have been more confident. It’s frustrating because I know I put a lot of work into the project, but all I could focus on was that moment when I stumbled. I just felt like I let myself down, and it made me doubt my abilities. I wish I could just shake off those negative thoughts, but they linger.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that moment really overshadowed all the hard work you put into your project, and it’s tough to feel embarrassed and let down by yourself. It’s understandable that those negative thoughts would linger, especially when you’re already feeling anxious about how others perceive you. It seems like there’s a lot of pressure you put on yourself to perform well. What do you think would help you start to shift those negative thoughts, even just a little bit?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess if I could remind myself that one presentation doesn’t define my worth or abilities, that might help. But it’s hard to believe that when I’m in the moment. Maybe if I could find a way to focus on the positives, like the effort I put in or any good feedback I received, it might make a difference. I just don’t know how to do that consistently. It feels like my mind always goes back to the negative stuff.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It’s great that you’re recognizing the importance of reminding yourself that one presentation doesn’t define your worth. Focusing on the positives, like your effort and any good feedback, sounds like a constructive way to shift your perspective. It’s completely normal for our minds to gravitate toward the negative, especially in stressful situations. 

Finding ways to consistently remind yourself of those positives can be a journey. What do you think could be a small step you could take to start practicing that shift in focus?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could try keeping a journal or something, where I write down the good things that happen at work or any positive feedback I get. It might help to have something to look back on when I’m feeling down. I just worry that I won’t stick with it or that I’ll forget to write in it. But I think it could be a helpful way to remind myself of the positives.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
A journal sounds like a wonderful idea! It could serve as a tangible reminder of your accomplishments and positive experiences. It’s understandable to worry about sticking with it, but even starting small—like writing just a few lines when you feel inspired—can be beneficial. 

What if you set a specific time or trigger for journaling, like at the end of your workday or after a meeting? That might help make it feel more manageable. How does that sound to you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: That sounds like a good idea. If I set a specific time, it might help me remember to do it. Maybe I could try writing a few lines at the end of the day about something positive that happened or something I felt proud of. I think that could help me focus on the good stuff instead of just the negatives. I’ll give it a shot and see how it goes. Thanks for the suggestion!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I’m glad that idea resonates with you! Setting a specific time to reflect on the positives can really help create a habit. It sounds like you’re taking a proactive step toward shifting your focus, which is great. Remember to be gentle with yourself as you try this out—it's okay if it takes time to adjust. 

If you ever want to share how it’s going or explore anything else, I’m here for you. Is there anything else on your mind today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I appreciate that. I think I just need some time to process everything we talked about. It feels good to have some ideas to work with, but I’m still a bit hesitant about sharing too much. I’ll try the journaling and see how that goes. Thanks for listening and being understanding. It really helps.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I completely understand, and it’s perfectly okay to take your time to process everything. I’m glad to hear that you found our conversation helpful and that you’re willing to try the journaling. Remember, you can share as much or as little as you feel comfortable with whenever you’re ready. 

Thank you for being open today, Morgan. I’m here whenever you want to talk again. Take care of yourself!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you. I appreciate your understanding and support. I’ll definitely keep that in mind. Take care, and I’ll see you next time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Morgan. I’m glad I could be here for you. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: See you soon! Thank you again.

This indicates a shift in perspective and a movement toward understanding underlying patterns, which aligns with the criteria for very high facilitation. However, while the questions were effective, they did not yet reach the level of challenging assumptions or introducing completely new angles that would characterize even higher facilitation.
